title: Podman in HyperV mode breaks on logout
body:
### Issue Description

After starting a hyper-v podman machine and logging out, on subsequent logins podman will be unable to connect to the machine, and unable to run `podman machine start`. Podman seems to be tied to a specific user logon session, but the hyper-v VM it spins up is not. This means that on logout, `gvproxy` gets terminated, but the hyper-v vm is still running.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Run `podman machine init` with podman in hyper-v mode
2. Run `podman machine start`
3. Log out
4. Log back in
5. Run `podman machine start`
6. Run `podman ps`

### Describe the results you received

> Starting machine "podman-machine-default"
podman : Error: unable to start "podman-machine-default": already running 

 > Error: unable to connect to Podman socket: failed to connect: dial tcp 127.0.0.1:65139: connectex: No connection could be made because the target machine actively refused it.
 podman : Cannot connect to Podman. Please verify your connection to the Linux system using `podman system connection list`, or try `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` to manage a new Linux VM

### Describe the results you expected

> Starting machine "podman-machine-default"
podman : Error: unable to start "podman-machine-default": already running 

> podman ps
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE       COMMAND     CREATED     STATUS      PORTS       NAMES

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

The host machine is a hyper-v vm with nested virtualization enabled. 

### Additional information

This also appears to happen when `podman machine start` is invoked from a service. The trigger for it to break isn't clear, but it seems to break quite quickly.

comments.body:
This is interesting... we could add a check to the start cmd and set gvproxy up again if it is down. 

The alternatives would be to try to shut the podman machine down on logout but not sure how feasible it is as the "sign out" happens quickly. Otherwise we should convert gvproxy to a service, but this requires admin privileges so it's not an option as we're trying to remove them.

BTW this is a problem as without gvproxy we lose connectivity and also volumes, so if there is some long running task active it will start failing.

I'm not sure how reasonable it is to not require admin privileges for managing system wide processes like hyper-v. Perhaps an approach similar to the one taken [here](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26277) makes sense? On the first `podman machine init`, set up some sort of service that handles gvproxy, and then reuse that for future `podman machine init` runs. This at least doesn't add any new admin requirements, but it does add a decent amount of complexity. As far as I can tell though, this general approach is how all the other prebuilt integration services work.

i've been toying with the idea machine needs some special error handling that would look for very specific errors and either give a very specific error message or take action.  For example, if a helper app takes a dump; could we restart the helper app or at the very minimum, recommend a stop and start.  I'm not sure how this helps here but maybe the concept is also applicable. Ofc changing the wiring underneath is a possibility as long as we are able to retain compat between minor verions.

> i've been toying with the idea machine needs some special error handling that would look for very specific errors and either give a very specific error message or take action. For example, if a helper app takes a dump; could we restart the helper app or at the very minimum, recommend a stop and start.

Yeah, i was thinking at something similar. If `gvproxy` is down but the VM is still running, we could catch that error state and either display a message like "gvproxy stopped unexpectedly. Run 'podman machine start' to re-attach networking" or simply attempt to restart the helper process automatically.

However, @cpr-nwright makes a fair point. But I suspect many users won't appreciate the increased attack surface or the requirement for Admin privileges.

What's your take on this @l0rd ? 

We could also keep the current user-level process as the default (by implementing the "self-fixing" logic) and also introduce a --persistent/as-service/whatever flag during `init` that configures gvproxy to run as a Windows Service, only for that specific machine.
I agree with @baude. We have invested in a "service-less" solution and we can improve it to catch helpers' failures. This problem doesn't justify introducing a Windows service, in my opinion.

If @lstocchi isn't mistaken this isn't just an issue with helper failures. This will also break running containers. That seems like a fairly major issue, and one that can't be fixed with some error handling logic in the helpers. That being said, some error handling logic in the helpers would at least solve my immediate problem.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
